title: Enforcing the Arbitral Award
primary_topics: rule of law as weapon of the weak
sub_topics: Cesar Bengzon as Filipino ICJ judge

stances.claim: Poor and weak states can vie with wealthy and powerful countries only through the might of being right — a steadfast invocation of the rule of law.
stances.rhetorical_move: aphoristic restatement of his rule-of-law-as-leverage thesis + appeal to his own teaching authority

stances.claim: The Philippines should pursue ICJ adjudication alongside continued peaceful methods — the ICJ is structurally superior to arbitration (permanent body, 9-year secure tenure judges, vs ad hoc tribunal that dissolves).
A well-crafted ICJ petition would not reopen the Arbitral Award — maritime rights are already won under UNCLOS and should not be re-litigated.
stances.rhetorical_move: doctrinal reassurance via textual reply to Guevarra's concern

stances.claim: Forcible modes (boycott, blockade, embargo, severance of diplomatic ties, armed encounters) are extremely difficult to win with — peaceful methods remain the realistic pathway.

China will not readily give up its preferred one-on-one negotiation mode, where it enjoys absolute superiority, to litigate as an equal in the ICJ — and the ICJ Statute requires China's consent for jurisdiction.
stances.rhetorical_move: structural-political analysis of Chinese incentives + jurisdictional realism

cases: Republic of the Philippines v. People's Republic of China (2016 Arbitral Award)
laws_treaties: United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS)

signature_phrases: Stick to the rule of law
register_markers: As a professor of public international law for many years in the 1960s
I have proposed several times in this space
I will detail my humble suggestions in a future column
text-message exchange quoted verbatim as anecdotal authority
decision_framework_signals: applies rule-of-law-as-asymmetric-weapon framework: weak states' only leverage against the powerful is legal-procedural
frames forcible enforcement modes as categorically unrealistic, narrowing pathway to peaceful options
uses structural institutional analysis (ICJ vs arbitration) to argue for escalation
uses jurisdictional-realism lens to acknowledge that China's consent is the hard constraint
cross_references: I have proposed several times in this space
notable_anecdotes: Justice Secretary Menardo Guevarra texting him for clarification on whether ICJ filing would reopen the Arbitral Award
Serving as chair of the Philippine National Group in the PCA alongside Pangalangan, Puno, and Vitug, nominating Iwasawa to the ICJ
